t: Objecting to bail — full legal reference
b:
OBJECTING TO BAIL — Legal Reference (Republic of Ireland)

1. CONSTITUTIONAL & FOUNDATIONAL

People (AG) v O'Callaghan [1966] IR 501 — Bail may only be refused to secure the accused's attendance at trial or to prevent interference with evidence or witnesses. Preventive detention (refusing bail because further offences may be committed) is unconstitutional.
Apply: your baseline objection is built on flight-risk and interference.

Ryan v DPP [1989] IR 399 — Affirmed O'Callaghan; likelihood of further offending was not a lawful ground before 1997.
Apply: shows the gap the 16th Amendment filled — useful for explaining why s.2 exists.

People (AG) v Gilliland [1985] IR 643 — Seriousness of the charge alone is not enough; there must be a real risk of flight.
Apply: "It's a serious charge" is not a standalone objection — tie it to genuine flight risk.

2. THE STATUTORY FRAMEWORK

Sixteenth Amendment (1996) — Article 40.4.6°: provision may be made by law for refusal of bail to a person charged with a serious offence where reasonably considered necessary to prevent the commission of a serious offence by that person.

Bail Act 1997 — key provisions
s.2 — bail may be refused for a "serious offence" (Schedule 1; punishable by 5 years or more) where refusal is reasonably considered necessary to prevent the commission of a serious offence by that person.
   • s.3 assault (causing harm) and burglary / aggravated burglary ARE Schedule 1 serious offences — s.2 objection available.
   • s.2 common assault and s.4 / s.6 Public Order are NOT — you are confined to O'Callaghan grounds only.
s.2(3) — the court need not be satisfied that a specific offence is intended.
s.4 — previous convictions must not be referred to in a manner prejudicing fair trial; proceedings may be heard otherwise than in public.
s.6 — conditions: residence, sign-on at a named station, no-contact.
s.7 — a member may give opinion/belief evidence that refusal is necessary to prevent a serious offence.
s.9 — power of arrest for breach of a bail condition.

s.2(2) — FACTORS THE COURT MUST WEIGH
(a) nature and degree of seriousness of the offence charged, and likely sentence
(b) nature and degree of seriousness of the offence apprehended, and likely sentence
(c) nature and strength of the evidence in support of the charge
(d) any conviction for an offence committed while on bail
(e) any previous convictions, including a conviction under appeal not yet determined
(f) any other offence for which the accused is charged and awaiting trial
(i) where one of the above applies — whether the accused is addicted to a controlled drug
(ii) the extent to which number and frequency of previous serious convictions indicate persistent serious offending
(iii) nature and likelihood of any danger to life, personal safety or the community (offence punishable by 10 years+)

3. FLIGHT-RISK / ATTENDANCE OBJECTIONS (O'Callaghan grounds)

O'Callaghan factors: strength of the evidence · severity of likely sentence · ties to the jurisdiction · prior failures to appear.
DPP v Corbally — seriousness of charge plus weak community ties supports a flight-risk objection. (Verify citation before filing.)
A prior failure to answer bail, or an outstanding/previously executed bench warrant, is itself a recognised indicator of flight risk — always surface it where present.

4. SECTION 2 (PREVENTIVE) OBJECTIONS — LEADING MODERN CASES

DPP v McLoughlin [2009] IESC 65 — The objecting Garda's belief must have a stated, genuine evidential basis; the court assesses the real risk of further serious offending; hearsay is admissible at the bail hearing.
Apply: your key authority — s.2 belief evidence must be REASONED, not bare assertion.

Vickers v DPP [2009] IESC 58 — The member's belief can be tested in cross-examination and must be reasonably held.
Apply: be ready to justify the basis of your belief when defence probes it.

Broderick v DPP [2006] 1 IR 629 — Presumption of innocence remains central; refusal must rest on substantial evidence.

DPP v Doyle — persistent serious offending (s.2(2)(ii)) can ground a preventive objection. (Verify citation.)

5. FAIR PROCEDURES — HOW THE OBJECTION MUST BE RUN

McDonagh v Governor of Cloverhill Prison [2005] IESC 4 — The accused is entitled to notice of the objections and a fair opportunity to meet them; detention without disclosure of grounds is unlawful.
Apply: disclose your grounds to the defence in advance — do not ambush.

DPP v McGinley [1998] 2 IR 408 — Bail conditions must be reasonable and proportionate, not a disguised refusal. (Verify citation.)

6. APPLIED TO ASSAULT SUSPECTS

Which ground applies: for s.3 (and s.4) assault you have BOTH routes — O'Callaghan and s.2. For s.2 common assault or s.4/s.6 Public Order, you are limited to O'Callaghan.

INTERFERENCE — your strongest O'Callaghan angle. Assault cases turn on one injured party or a handful of witnesses. Where there is a real risk the accused will approach, intimidate or pressure them — domestic, neighbour, or parties known to each other — that is a lawful freestanding ground even on a non-serious charge.
   • Point to history between accused and victim (prior incidents, PULSE call-outs, protection/barring orders).
   • Point to proximity — same address, same estate, shared workplace.
   • Where interference risk is real but bail likely: press for conditions — no-contact (s.6), stay-away from a named address, sign-on.

s.2 ANGLE FOR s.3 ASSAULT: where there is a pattern of violent offending, run s.2 on danger to personal safety / the community — s.2(2)(iii) is directly in point for offences punishable by 10 years+ (s.4 assault); s.2(2)(ii) supports the persistent-offender argument.

7. BREACH — "BAIL ON TOP OF BAIL"

s.11 Criminal Justice Act 1984 — an offence committed while on bail carries a CONSECUTIVE sentence. Cite when objecting to a fresh charge committed while already on bail — strengthens both s.2 and the sentencing position.
s.9 Bail Act 1997 — power of arrest for breach of a condition.

8. APPLIED TO BURGLARY SUSPECTS

Criminal Justice (Burglary of Dwellings) Act 2015 (amending s.2): where an adult is charged with a relevant dwelling-burglary offence and has the specified recent burglary record, the court SHALL consider those circumstances as evidence that the person is likely to commit a relevant offence in a dwelling.

THE PRESUMPTION — CHECKLIST (all must be present):
   • A "relevant offence" — burglary or aggravated burglary (ss.12–13 CJ(T&FO)A 2001) in a DWELLING, accused aged 18+.
   • EITHER a prior conviction for a relevant dwelling offence within 5 years before the current alleged offence; OR two or more relevant offences (convictions or charges awaiting trial) within the window from 6 months before to 6 months after the current alleged offence.
   • A conviction under appeal (not determined or withdrawn) still counts.

HOW TO USE IT: don't just assert he's a burglar — lay out the SPECIFIC prior dwelling-burglary conviction(s) and dates, and the current dwelling charge, so the court sees the statutory trigger is met. Combine with s.2(2)(ii) where the record is extensive.

WHERE IT DOESN'T APPLY: first-time burglary, non-dwelling (commercial) burglary, or a juvenile. Fall back on ordinary s.2 (burglary is still Schedule 1) and O'Callaghan flight-risk — transient lifestyle, no fixed abode, bench-warrant history.

9. WHERE THE ACCUSED IS HOMELESS / NO FIXED ABODE

CORE PRINCIPLE: homelessness is NOT of itself a ground to refuse bail. Refusing bail solely for lack of an address punishes housing status and cuts against the presumption of bail and Article 40 liberty rights. It is relevant only insofar as it feeds a lawful objection — principally flight risk.

WHERE IT BITES: no fixed abode goes to ties to the jurisdiction and the practicality of locating the accused. Run it as "no fixed abode, COMBINED WITH [prior bench warrants / failures to appear / no community ties / no employment / transient movement]" — never "no fixed abode, therefore refuse."

THE PRACTICAL PROBLEM — CONDITIONS: s.6(1)(b) allows residence and sign-on conditions. A homeless accused often cannot satisfy a residence condition. The court then leans on sign-on, curfew or a surety. If the accused proposes a hostel or family address, that becomes the residence condition and much of the flight-risk edge falls away — anticipate it.

Homelessness does not change the s.2 analysis, which turns on risk of further serious offending, not where the person lives.

CITATION TABLE
People (AG) v O'Callaghan — [1966] IR 501 (SC)
Ryan v DPP — [1989] IR 399 (SC)
People (AG) v Gilliland — [1985] IR 643 (SC)
DPP v McLoughlin — [2009] IESC 65
Vickers v DPP — [2009] IESC 58
Broderick v DPP — [2006] 1 IR 629
McDonagh v Governor of Cloverhill Prison — [2005] IESC 4
DPP v McGinley — [1998] 2 IR 408 (verify)
DPP v Corbally — (verify)
DPP v Doyle — (verify)

Entries marked verify are included for the point of principle — confirm the neutral citation before relying on them in a file or bail objection.

THE PRÉCIS OF EVIDENCE

A Detailed Practitioner's Guide to Construction

Purpose · Structure · Persuasive Effect on the Accused · Irish Case Law

Prepared for An Garda Síochána court-file preparation

DMR North Central · Mountjoy Garda Station

Contents

1. What a Précis of Evidence Is

A précis of evidence (also called a summary of evidence or statement of
the facts) is a concise, structured narrative that draws together every
strand of the investigation into a single coherent account of what the
prosecution says happened, who did it, and how each element of the
offence is proven. It is not itself evidence; it is a working
distillation of the evidence that already exists in the book of
evidence, statements, exhibits and technical material.

Its function is to let a reader — a supervisor, the Director of Public
Prosecutions, prosecuting counsel, or the defence solicitor — grasp the
whole case in a few pages without reading every underlying document. A
good précis answers, in order: the charge, the parties, the sequence of
events, the evidential source for each material fact, and the conclusion
that the elements of the offence are satisfied.

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Distinguish four related documents                                    |
|                                                                       |
| • Précis / summary of evidence — the investigator's structured        |
| narrative of the whole case (this guide).                             |
|                                                                       |
| • Book of evidence — the formal statutory bundle served under s.4B    |
| Criminal Procedure Act 1967 in indictable cases.                      |
|                                                                       |
| • Statement of facts — the short account read to the court on a       |
| guilty plea or at sentencing.                                         |
|                                                                       |
| • Covering (cover) report — the report to the DPP or Superintendent   |
| recommending charge/direction, to which the précis is usually         |
| attached.                                                             |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

2. Why It Matters

-   Decision-making: It is the primary document on which a charge
    decision or DPP direction is made. A weak précis produces a weak
    direction.

-   Disclosure discipline: Building it forces the investigator to
    confront gaps, inconsistencies and disclosure obligations early,
    while they can still be cured.

-   Case theory: It crystallises the prosecution's theory of the case —
    the single explanatory account that ties the evidence together.

-   Plea leverage: When served or summarised to the defence, a
    tightly-built précis demonstrates the strength of the case and is
    often the single most powerful driver of an early guilty plea (see
    Part 6).

3. Before You Draft: Assembling the Material

Never draft from memory. Assemble and index the full evidential base
first, then write to it. The précis is only as reliable as the source
material behind each sentence.

3.1 The evidence audit

1.  Injured party / complainant statement(s).

2.  Witness statements — civilian and Garda.

3.  Accused's account: memo of interview, cautioned statement, or record
    of 'no comment'.

4.  Real and documentary exhibits with continuity (seizure, custody,
    exhibit numbers).

5.  Technical evidence: CCTV, forensics, telecoms, medical/injury
    reports, mapping.

6.  Custody record and detention authorisations (relevant to
    admissibility).

7.  Antecedents / previous convictions (kept separate from the
    narrative).

3.2 Prove the elements before you write

Identify the offence and break it into its constituent legal elements.
For each element, name the precise evidential source that proves it. If
any element has no source, that is a charge problem, not a drafting
problem.

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Element to be proven     Evidential source (example: s.3 assault
                           causing harm)
  ------------------------ ----------------------------------------------
  Assault / application of Injured party statement; independent
  force                    eyewitness; CCTV capturing the strike

  Causing harm             Medical report describing injury; photographs
                           of injuries; A&E records

  Identity of accused      Recognition/identification evidence; CCTV
                           continuity; admissions in interview

  Intention or             Circumstances of the act; words used;
  recklessness             interview answers; nature of the force

  Absence of lawful excuse Complainant account negativing
                           consent/self-defence; scene evidence
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

4. Structure of the Précis

A disciplined structure makes the case legible and self-auditing. Use
the following order. Each section is short; the whole document is
typically two to six pages.

4.1 Heading block

-   Station, division and PULSE incident number.

-   Accused: full name, date of birth, address.

-   Charge(s): exact wording, statute and section, and date/place of
    offence.

-   Investigating member and date of report.

4.2 Introduction

One short paragraph: who the accused is, what offence is alleged, and
the date, time and place. State the charge in a single sentence so the
reader is oriented immediately.

4.3 Chronological narrative of the facts

The core of the précis. Tell the story in strict chronological order, in
the third person and the past tense, in plain and neutral language.
Attribute every material fact to its source as you go — do not assert
facts the evidence cannot support.

-   Neutral tone: state what the evidence shows, not what you believe.
    Avoid emotive adjectives and conclusions of law within the
    narrative.

-   Attribution: e.g. 'The injured party states that…', 'CCTV from
    premises X shows…', 'In the course of interview the accused
    admitted…'.

-   One fact per source: if a fact rests on two sources, cite both; if
    it rests on none, delete it.

-   Time and place anchors: pin each event to a time and location so the
    sequence is testable against CCTV and telecoms.

4.4 The accused's account

Summarise fairly what the accused said in interview or by cautioned
statement, including any exculpatory account, denial or 'no comment'.
Fair treatment of the defence account is not weakness — it protects the
file from a later disclosure or fairness challenge and signals to a
reviewing lawyer that the case will withstand scrutiny.

4.5 Analysis of the evidence against the elements

Take each element of the offence in turn and state, in a sentence or
two, which evidence proves it. This is the section that converts a story
into a prosecutable case and it is where the persuasive force of the
document is concentrated.

4.6 Anticipated defences and evidential weaknesses

Identify realistically what the defence will run — self-defence,
consent, mistaken identity, intoxication, alibi — and note the evidence
that answers each. Candidly flag any weakness (gap in continuity,
single-witness identification, delay). A précis that hides its
weaknesses misleads the decision-maker and collapses on contact with the
defence.

4.7 Exhibits and continuity schedule

A short schedule listing each exhibit, its number, who seized it, and
the continuity chain. This demonstrates the physical case is intact and
admissible.

4.8 Conclusion and recommendation

State plainly that the evidence establishes each element and that a
prosecution is warranted (or set out the direction sought). Keep it to a
few sentences; the analysis has already done the work.

5. Drafting Principles

-   Accuracy over advocacy: the précis must be scrupulously accurate.
    Overstatement is fatal — it is disclosable and can be put to the
    investigator in cross-examination.

-   Brevity with completeness: include every material fact and no
    immaterial ones. If a sentence does not advance an element or answer
    a defence, cut it.

-   Plain English: short sentences, active voice, no jargon the DPP or a
    judge would not use.

-   Admissibility awareness: flag any evidence whose admissibility is
    contested (detention, search, identification, confession) so the
    reader can weigh it.

-   Separate fact from comment: the narrative is fact; analysis and
    recommendation are clearly signposted as such.

-   Consistency: names, times, exhibit numbers and spellings must match
    the underlying statements exactly.

6. How the Précis Can Persuade the Accused to Plead Guilty

A précis has no coercive power and must never be used to pressure or
induce a plea by threat or promise — an induced confession or plea is
liable to be excluded and can taint the prosecution. Its legitimate
persuasive effect is different and entirely proper: by presenting the
strength and coherence of the lawfully-gathered case clearly and early,
it allows the accused and their solicitor to make a rational, informed
decision that the case is unwinnable and that an early plea is in the
accused's own interest.

6.1 The mechanisms of legitimate persuasion

8.  Demonstrated cohesion: a well-built précis shows the defence that
    every element is independently proven, removing the hope that one
    weak link collapses the case.

9.  Independent corroboration: where CCTV, forensics or independent
    witnesses converge with the complainant's account, the précis makes
    that convergence visible and the defence's room to manoeuvre
    shrinks.

10. The accused's own words: admissions or damaging inconsistencies from
    interview, fairly summarised, are frequently decisive in the
    defence's own risk assessment.

11. Answered defences: by pre-empting self-defence, consent or identity,
    the précis signals that the obvious defences are already closed off.

12. Sentencing incentive: an early plea attracts a discount in sentence,
    and a defence solicitor reading a strong précis will advise the
    client accordingly.

6.2 The lawful boundary

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Persuasion vs. inducement — the line that must not be crossed         |
|                                                                       |
| LEGITIMATE: presenting the true strength of the lawfully-obtained     |
| evidence so the accused can assess their position realistically.      |
|                                                                       |
| UNLAWFUL: offering favours, threats, or promises to secure a plea or  |
| admission; overstating the evidence; withholding material that        |
| undermines the case.                                                  |
|                                                                       |
| An admission or plea obtained by inducement is liable to exclusion    |
| and undermines the very prosecution the précis was meant to support.  |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

6.3 The sentencing-discount context

Irish sentencing law expressly rewards an early plea, and this is the
lawful engine behind plea persuasion. The discount is greatest for a
plea entered at the earliest opportunity, and the value of a strong
précis is that it lets the defence reach that decision early, when the
discount is largest. The leading authorities are set out in Part 7.

6.4 Practical drafting choices that increase persuasive force

-   Lead the element-analysis with the single strongest piece of
    independent evidence.

-   Where an admission exists, quote the material words precisely and
    attribute them to the interview record.

-   Present the continuity schedule so no exhibit looks vulnerable to a
    s.4 / chain-of-custody challenge.

-   State candidly but briefly why the anticipated defences fail on the
    evidence.

-   Keep it factual and unemotional — a sober, accurate précis is far
    more persuasive to a solicitor than an argumentative one.

7. Relevant Case Law

The authorities below bear on the two things a précis must respect: the
admissibility of the evidence it summarises, and the lawful basis on
which an early plea is encouraged and rewarded.

7.1 Confessions, admissions and the voluntariness of a plea

Because a précis often turns on the accused's own admissions, and
because plea persuasion must never tip into inducement, the
confession-voluntariness authorities are central.

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Authority               Court / Year    Principle relevant to the
                                          précis
  ----------------------- --------------- -------------------------------
  People (AG) v Cummins   SC [1972] IR    A confession must be voluntary;
                          312             a statement obtained by threat
                                          or inducement is inadmissible.
                                          Governs how admissions may be
                                          summarised and used.

  People (DPP) v Shaw     SC [1982] IR 1  Fundamental fairness and the
                                          voluntariness test for
                                          admissions; the court's
                                          discretion to exclude unfairly
                                          obtained statements.

  People (DPP) v Pringle  CCA (1981) 2    Reliability and voluntariness
                          Frewen 57       of admissions; scrutiny of the
                                          circumstances in which they
                                          were obtained.

  People (DPP) v Gormley  SC [2014] IESC  Right of access to a solicitor
  & White                 17              before/during questioning;
                                          admissions taken in breach may
                                          be excluded — directly affects
                                          what interview material a
                                          précis can rely on.

  People (DPP) v Doyle    SC [2017] IESC  Detailed treatment of the right
                          1               to legal advice during
                                          custodial questioning and the
                                          consequences for admissibility
                                          of admissions.
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

7.2 Exclusion of unconstitutionally or unlawfully obtained evidence

A précis must flag evidence of doubtful admissibility. The current test
governs whether searches, seizures and detentions will survive
challenge.

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Authority               Court / Year    Principle relevant to the
                                          précis
  ----------------------- --------------- -------------------------------
  People (DPP) v JC       SC [2015] IESC  Reformulated the exclusionary
                          31              rule: unconstitutionally
                                          obtained evidence may be
                                          admitted absent
                                          deliberate/conscious or
                                          reckless breach. The key modern
                                          test the précis must apply to
                                          seized/searched material.

  People (AG) v O'Brien   SC [1965] IR    Origin of the exclusionary rule
                          142             for unconstitutionally obtained
                                          evidence; the pre-JC baseline.

  People (DPP) v Kenny    SC [1990] 2 IR  The strict pre-JC 'absolute
                          110             protection' rule — essential
                                          context for JC and still cited
                                          on the development of the
                                          doctrine.

  Damache v DPP           SC [2012] IESC  Search warrant issued by a
                          11              member not independent of the
                                          investigation was invalid;
                                          continuity and lawfulness of
                                          searches must be shown in the
                                          exhibits schedule.
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

7.3 Identification evidence

Where identity is summarised, the précis should reflect the caution the
courts require.

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Authority               Court / Year    Principle relevant to the
                                          précis
  ----------------------- --------------- -------------------------------
  People (AG) v Casey     SC [1963] IR 33 The Casey warning: juries must
  (No.2)                                  be warned of the dangers of
                                          visual identification evidence.
                                          Flags single-witness ID as a
                                          weakness to note in the précis.

  People (DPP) v O'Reilly CCA [1990] 2 IR Application of identification
                          415             safeguards and the need for
                                          care with recognition/ID
                                          evidence.
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

7.4 The guilty plea and sentencing discount

These authorities are the lawful basis on which an early, informed plea
is encouraged — the legitimate engine of plea persuasion discussed in
Part 6.

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Authority               Court / Year    Principle relevant to the
                                          précis
  ----------------------- --------------- -------------------------------
  People (DPP) v Tiernan  SC [1988] IR    Recognises the mitigating value
                          250             of a guilty plea; foundational
                                          on plea as mitigation in
                                          serious cases.

  People (DPP) v G.K. /   CCA [2008]      Discount for a plea depends on
  plea principles         IECCA 110       its timing and value; the
                                          earlier and more useful the
                                          plea, the greater the credit.

  People (DPP) v Z (early sentencing      A plea at the earliest
  plea)                   principle       opportunity attracts the
                                          fullest discount; the practical
                                          incentive a strong précis lets
                                          the defence realise early.

  People (DPP) v M        SC [1994] 3 IR  Sentencing must reflect
                          306             mitigating factors including a
                                          plea; the court weighs the plea
                                          in fixing the proportionate
                                          sentence.
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

Note: case citations should be verified against BAILII or courts.ie
before inclusion in a live file. The plea-discount cases in particular
are applied through a body of Court of Appeal sentencing decisions that
evolve; confirm the current leading authority for the specific offence.

8. Final Checklist

1.  Charge stated exactly, with statute and section.

2.  Every element of the offence matched to a named evidential source.

3.  Narrative chronological, third person, past tense, neutral.

4.  Each material fact attributed to its source.

5.  Accused's account summarised fairly, including any denial or 'no
    comment'.

6.  Anticipated defences identified and answered.

7.  Weaknesses candidly flagged, not concealed.

8.  Admissibility issues (detention, search, ID, confession) noted.

9.  Exhibits and continuity scheduled.

10. No inducement, threat or overstatement anywhere in the file.

11. Names, times, exhibit numbers consistent with underlying statements.

12. Conclusion states the elements are met and the direction sought.

This guide is a general practitioner aid. It is not legal advice and
does not replace the Garda Síochána Investigation Manual, DPP
guidelines, or verified case law. Confirm all citations before use in a
live prosecution file.

INVESTIGATION & PROSECUTION GUIDE

PUBLIC ORDER — SS. 6 & 8

Threatening, Abusive or Insulting Behaviour • Failure to Comply with a
Direction

Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994

These are among the most commonly prosecuted offences in the District
Court. Often the only evidence is the oral testimony of the arresting
member, so precision in the note, the caution, and the sequence of
events is everything. Build both files to survive a defence that turns
on procedure.

PART A — Section 6: Threatening, Abusive or Insulting Behaviour

1. The Offence

s.6(1): it is an offence for any person in a public place to use or
engage in any threatening, abusive or insulting words or behaviour, with
intent to provoke a breach of the peace or being reckless as to whether
a breach of the peace may be occasioned.

Penalty (s.6(2), as amended by the Intoxicating Liquor Act 2008):
summary only — a class D fine (up to €1,000), or imprisonment for a term
not exceeding 3 months, or both.

2. The Elements / Proofs

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| PROOF CHECKLIST — s.6                                                 |
|                                                                       |
| 1.  A person (the accused, identified).                               |
|                                                                       |
| 2.  In a public place — see the statutory definition below (s.3).     |
|                                                                       |
| 3.  Used or engaged in words OR behaviour — note s.6 catches words    |
|     alone, unlike affray. Record the actual words/conduct verbatim.   |
|                                                                       |
| 4.  That were threatening, abusive or insulting — objective quality   |
|     of the words/behaviour.                                           |
|                                                                       |
| 5.  Mental element — either (a) intent to provoke a breach of the     |
|     peace, OR (b) recklessness as to whether a breach of the peace    |
|     may be occasioned.                                                |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

3. "Public place" — statutory definition (s.3)

s.3 of the 1994 Act defines "public place" broadly. It includes: any
highway; any outdoor area to which the public have access (whether as of
right, by permission, on payment, or otherwise); any cemetery or
churchyard; and any premises or place to which the public have access
(again as of right, by permission, on payment or otherwise), including
any train, vessel or vehicle used to carry persons for reward.

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| PRACTICAL POINT — prove the locus is a "public place"                 |
|                                                                       |
| Do not assume it. State expressly in the statement that the locus was |
| a public place and why (a public road/footpath, a licensed premises   |
| open to the public, a bus, etc.). A charge can fail on this element   |
| alone if the evidence is silent.                                      |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

4. "Breach of the peace" — the key concept

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Thorpe v DPP [2006] IEHC — Irish position                             |
|                                                                       |
| Breach of the peace is not statutorily defined in Irish law. The      |
| Irish courts have acknowledged the absence of a domestic definition   |
| and looked to the settled common-law meaning.                         |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| R v Howell [1982] QB 416 — persuasive (the working definition)        |
|                                                                       |
| A breach of the peace arises where harm is actually done or likely to |
| be done to a person, or (in his presence) to his property, or where a |
| person is put in fear of such harm through an assault, an affray, a   |
| riot, unlawful assembly or other disturbance. The essence is actual   |
| or imminent violence — not mere annoyance or offence.                 |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

Consequence for charging: s.6 requires a nexus to violence (actual or
apprehended). Purely offensive or annoying conduct that carries no risk
of violence sits better under s.5 (offensive conduct) than s.6. Don't
over-charge annoyance as s.6.

5. Threatening / abusive / insulting — the objective limb

-   The words or behaviour must objectively be threatening, abusive or
    insulting — record them exactly (verbatim, including profanity) in
    the note and statement. Paraphrase weakens the proof.

-   "Behaviour" covers gestures and physical conduct, not just speech.

-   Free-expression caution: rudeness, or speech that merely offends, is
    not automatically criminal. The prosecution must still prove the
    breach-of-the-peace mental element. Build the evidence of the
    volatile context (crowd, provocation, likelihood of a fight).

6. Mental element — intent OR recklessness

Two alternative routes, and either suffices:

-   Intent to provoke a breach of the peace; or

-   Recklessness — the accused was aware of a risk that a breach of the
    peace might be occasioned and went on anyway. This is the more
    common route in practice.

Evidence the mental element from context: who the words were directed
at, the reaction they drew, the presence of a rival group, prior
warnings, and the accused's demeanour. Note it all.

7. Investigation — s.6

-   Record the words/behaviour verbatim at the earliest moment
    (pocketbook, then statement).

-   State the locus and why it is a public place.

-   Capture the reaction of others present (fear, a gathering crowd,
    someone squaring up) — this evidences the breach-of-peace element.

-   Note intoxication observations (voluntary intoxication is not a
    defence to this basic-intent-style offence, but record it).

-   Preserve CCTV/body-worn footage — issue preservation requests
    promptly.

-   Note any prior s.8 direction given and whether it was complied with
    (this often runs alongside a s.8 charge).

8. Anticipated defences — s.6

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "It was just words / free speech."                                    |
|                                                                       |
| s.6 expressly covers words alone — but the breach-of-peace element    |
| must be proved. Lead the context showing a real risk of violence.     |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "The locus wasn't a public place."                                    |
|                                                                       |
| Meet with the s.3 definition and specific facts. Common failure point |
| — close it in the statement.                                          |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "No breach of the peace was likely."                                  |
|                                                                       |
| Evidence the volatile context, the target, and the reaction.          |
| Distinguish from mere annoyance (which is s.5 territory).             |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "I was too drunk to intend anything."                                 |
|                                                                       |
| Recklessness route: voluntary intoxication does not assist. And       |
| awareness of risk can be inferred from conduct.                       |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

PART B — Section 8: Failure to Comply with a Direction

1. The Offence

s.8 creates an offence of failing to comply with a lawful Garda
direction. The structure is two-stage: first a member must have a lawful
basis to give a direction (s.8(1)); then it is an offence to fail to
comply with it (s.8(2)).

Penalty (s.8(3), as amended): summary only — a class D fine (up to
€1,000), or imprisonment for a term not exceeding 6 months, or both.

2. Stage 1 — the lawful basis for a direction (s.8(1))

A member who finds a person in a public place may give a direction where
the member suspects, with reasonable cause, that the person:

-   (a) is or has been acting contrary to s.4, s.5, s.6, s.7 or s.9
    (intoxication; offensive conduct; threatening/abusive behaviour;
    distribution/display of offensive material; wilful obstruction); OR

-   (b) without lawful authority or reasonable excuse, is loitering in a
    public place in circumstances (which may include the company of
    others) that give rise to a reasonable apprehension for the safety
    of persons or property, or for the maintenance of the public peace.

The direction (s.8(1)(i)–(ii)) may require the person to do either or
both: (i) desist from acting in that manner; and (ii) leave the vicinity
immediately in a peaceable or orderly manner.

3. Stage 2 — the offence of non-compliance (s.8(2))

It is an offence to fail to comply without lawful authority or
reasonable excuse. The offence is the failure to comply with a validly
given direction — so if Stage 1 is defective, the s.8 charge falls.

4. Case Law — s.8 (Irish authority)

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| DPP v Galligan (1995) & Bates v Brady [2003] — THE WARNING            |
| REQUIREMENT                                                           |
|                                                                       |
| The superior courts have held that, to secure a conviction under s.8, |
| evidence must be given that the accused was informed that a failure   |
| to comply would constitute a criminal offence. The person must        |
| understand the consequence of non-compliance. Omitting this warning   |
| is fatal to the charge.                                               |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| DPP v Kelly [2018] IECA — the direction must rest on a proper         |
| statutory basis                                                       |
|                                                                       |
| The York Road / Dun Laoghaire case. The Court of Appeal examined      |
| whether the statutory prerequisites for a s.8 direction were met. Key |
| lessons: the member must actually suspect, with reasonable cause,     |
| conduct within one of the gateway offences (here s.9 obstruction);    |
| the direction and its basis must be properly grounded; and the        |
| sequence — direction, warning of consequences, opportunity to comply  |
| — must be evidenced. On the facts the members repeatedly gave the     |
| direction, explained the consequences, and allowed time to comply.    |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

Reading Galligan / Bates / Kelly together, a sound s.8 file shows, in
sequence:

6.  The member found the person in a public place;

7.  The member had reasonable cause to suspect a gateway offence
    (s.4/5/6/7/9) or qualifying loitering;

8.  A clear direction was given (desist and/or leave, peaceably);

9.  The person was told that failure to comply is a criminal offence;

10. A real opportunity to comply was given;

11. The person failed to comply, without lawful authority or reasonable
    excuse.

5. Common failure points — s.8

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Defect                  Effect
  ----------------------- -----------------------------------------------
  No warning of           Charge fails — Galligan / Bates v Brady. Must
  consequences            tell the person non-compliance is an offence.

  No / weak gateway       If the member cannot articulate reasonable
  suspicion               cause to suspect a s.4/5/6/7/9 offence or
                          qualifying loitering, the direction is unlawful
                          and s.8 falls.

  No opportunity to       The person must have a genuine chance to obey
  comply                  before the failure crystallises.

  Vague direction         "Move on" without clarity on what/where.
                          Direction must be capable of being complied
                          with.

  Not a public place      s.8(1) requires the person be found in a public
                          place (s.3 definition).

  Reasonable excuse       Statutory defence — the person had lawful
  present                 authority or reasonable excuse to remain.
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

6. Investigation / note-taking — s.8

-   Record the exact words of the direction given, and the exact words
    of the warning that non-compliance is an offence.

-   Record the reasonable-cause basis — what gateway conduct (or
    loitering apprehension) you suspected and why.

-   Note the time given to comply and the person's response.

-   Where multiple people are directed, identify each individually (as
    in Kelly — eight persons, individually identified).

-   Note s.24 name-and-address demand if made, and the response.

PART C — How ss. 6 and 8 Work Together

In the field these two offences frequently run in sequence. A person
behaving in a threatening/abusive way (potential s.6) is given a s.8
direction to desist and leave. If they refuse, you may have both a s.6
and a s.8 charge. Charge each on its own proofs — they are independent.

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| SEQUENCING — typical street scenario                                  |
|                                                                       |
| 12. Conduct observed — threatening/abusive words in a public place →  |
|     potential s.6.                                                    |
|                                                                       |
| 13. Direction given under s.8(1)(a) (suspected s.6 conduct): desist   |
|     and/or leave, peaceably.                                          |
|                                                                       |
| 14. Warning — tell them failure to comply is a criminal offence       |
|     (Galligan / Bates).                                               |
|                                                                       |
| 15. Opportunity to comply — give a real chance.                       |
|                                                                       |
| 16. Failure → s.8 charge; the underlying conduct may still ground a   |
|     separate s.6 charge.                                              |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

Quick Reference Card

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| s.6 — THREATENING / ABUSIVE / INSULTING BEHAVIOUR                     |
|                                                                       |
| -   Where: public place (s.3 definition).                             |
|                                                                       |
| -   Conduct: threatening, abusive OR insulting words OR behaviour     |
|     (words alone suffice).                                            |
|                                                                       |
| -   Mens rea: intent to provoke a breach of the peace OR recklessness |
|     as to same.                                                       |
|                                                                       |
| -   Breach of peace: undefined in Ireland (Thorpe v DPP); common-law  |
|     meaning — actual/imminent violence (R v Howell).                  |
|                                                                       |
| -   Penalty: summary; class D fine and/or 3 months.                   |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| s.8 — FAILURE TO COMPLY WITH A DIRECTION                              |
|                                                                       |
| -   Gateway (s.8(1)(a)): reasonable-cause suspicion of s.4, 5, 6, 7   |
|     or 9; OR (s.8(1)(b)) qualifying loitering.                        |
|                                                                       |
| -   Direction: desist and/or leave the vicinity, peaceably.           |
|                                                                       |
| -   MUST warn: tell the person failure to comply is an offence        |
|     (Galligan; Bates v Brady). Fatal if omitted.                      |
|                                                                       |
| -   Must give: a real opportunity to comply.                          |
|                                                                       |
| -   Defence: lawful authority or reasonable excuse.                   |
|                                                                       |
| -   Penalty: summary; class D fine and/or 6 months.                   |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

Internal aide-mémoire. Verify all citations against the eISB / revised
Acts and BAILII / courts.ie before use in a live file. R v Howell is
English (persuasive only); Thorpe v DPP, DPP v Galligan, Bates v Brady
and DPP v Kelly are Irish authority — confirm exact citations and
holdings before relying on them in court.

INVESTIGATION & PROSECUTION GUIDE

AFFRAY

Section 16, Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994

1. The Offence

Section 16(1) provides that where (a) two or more persons at any place
(public or private, or both) use or threaten to use violence towards
each other; (b) the violence so used or threatened by one of those
persons is unlawful; and (c) the conduct of those persons taken together
is such as would cause a person of reasonable firmness present at that
place to fear for his or another person's safety — then each such person
who uses or threatens to use unlawful violence is guilty of affray.

The common law offence of affray was abolished by s.16(5). This is now
the sole affray offence in the State.

Key statutory features (s.16(2)–(4))

-   Threat alone is not enough — for the threat to found the offence it
    cannot be made by the use of words alone (s.16(2)). A threat by
    words only will not do; there must be conduct.

-   No bystander need actually be present (s.16(3)) — the "person of
    reasonable firmness" is a hypothetical/notional test. No real person
    need be, or be likely to be, present.

-   Public or private place — unlike some POA offences, affray can occur
    wholly in a private place (e.g. inside a dwelling).

-   Two or more persons using/threatening violence towards each other —
    the violence is between the participants, not directed at innocent
    bystanders. A group fighting each other in the street is the
    paradigm case.

Penalty

-   On indictment: a fine, or imprisonment for a term not exceeding 5
    years, or both.

-   Note: Affray is a "serious offence" for the purposes of the Bail Act
    1997 (Schedule), so bail may be objected to on the ground of likely
    commission of a further serious offence. Restriction on movement
    orders (Criminal Justice Act 2006, s.101) and exclusion orders
    (CJ(PO) Act 2003) may also apply on conviction.

2. Proofs — What the DPP Must Establish

Break the file down against each element. Every proof needs an
evidential source identified.

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| PROOF CHECKLIST                                                       |
|                                                                       |
| 1.  Two or more persons used or threatened violence towards each      |
|     other.                                                            |
|                                                                       |
| 2.  Violence / threat of violence — actual violent conduct, or a      |
|     threat by conduct (not words alone).                              |
|                                                                       |
| 3.  Unlawfulness — the violence used/threatened by at least one       |
|     participant was unlawful (i.e. not justified by self-defence,     |
|     lawful arrest, etc.).                                             |
|                                                                       |
| 4.  Cumulative conduct testtaken together would cause a person of     |
|     reasonable firmness present to fear for their own or another's    |
|     safety.                                                           |
|                                                                       |
| 5.  Identification of the accused as one who used or threatened       |
|     unlawful violence.                                                |
|                                                                       |
| 6.  Mental element — the accused intended to use or threaten          |
|     violence, or was aware that his conduct may be violent / threaten |
|     violence (see s.16 read with the mens rea discussion below).      |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

Note on the "reasonable firmness" limb: it is assessed on the combined
conduct of the participants — a single push might not qualify, but the
same act as part of a wider melee may. Build the file to show the whole
scene, not just your accused's act in isolation.

3. The Mental Element

The Irish statute does not spell out a mens rea for affray, but affray
is treated as a basic-intent offence. The prosecution must prove the
accused intended to use or threaten violence, or was aware that his
conduct may be (or may threaten) violence.

Because it is a basic-intent offence, voluntary intoxication is not a
defence. A person who is voluntarily drunk or drugged cannot rely on
that state to negative the awareness element — see the intoxication
section below.

4. Case Law

IMPORTANT — read before citing: Irish reported authority specifically
construing s.16 is sparse. Much of the developed affray jurisprudence is
English, decided under the Public Order Act 1986 (ss.3, 8). The Irish
s.16 and the English s.3 are closely modelled on each other, so the
English cases are persuasive but not binding. Flag them as such in any
submission. The English general principles on intoxication and
self-defence, however, rest on House of Lords authority (Majewski) that
Irish courts have consistently applied.

(a) Nature and purpose of the offence

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| I v DPP [2002] 1 AC 285 (HL) — persuasive                             |
|                                                                       |
| Affray protects bystanders, not the participants. The offence is      |
| directed at the effect of the violence on others present (real or     |
| notional), not at protecting those engaged in the fight. Confirmed    |
| there must be a person against whom violence is used or threatened.   |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Leeson v DPP [2010] EWHC 994 (Admin) — persuasive                     |
|                                                                       |
| Where violence is focused solely and exclusively on one victim, such  |
| that no notional bystander of reasonable firmness would fear for      |
| their safety, affray is not made out. Practical point: distinguish    |
| affray from a straightforward assault — affray needs the wider "scene |
| of terror" quality.                                                   |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

(b) Violence against persons, not property

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| POA 1986, s.8 (definition) — persuasive on the Irish position         |
|                                                                       |
| In the English scheme, affray covers violent conduct towards persons  |
| only — not violence directed at property. The Irish s.16 language     |
| ("violence towards each other") points the same way. Damage to        |
| property during the incident may be relevant context (and may found   |
| separate criminal-damage charges) but the affray itself must rest on  |
| person-directed violence.                                             |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

(c) Intoxication

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| DPP v Majewski [1977] AC 443 (HL) — applied in Ireland                |
|                                                                       |
| Self-induced (voluntary) intoxication is no defence to a crime of     |
| basic intent. Becoming voluntarily intoxicated is itself a reckless   |
| course of conduct that supplies the necessary culpability. As affray  |
| is basic intent, an accused cannot say drink/drugs deprived him of    |
| awareness.                                                            |
|                                                                       |
| Irish courts have proceeded on Majewski lines — the specific/basic    |
| intent distinction and the treatment of voluntary intoxication is     |
| well established (see the Law Reform Commission's analysis, and the   |
| intoxication discussion in People (DPP) v Eadon [2019] IESC 98 on the |
| "actual formation of intent" test for specific-intent crimes, which   |
| by contrast does not assist a basic-intent charge like affray).       |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Involuntary intoxication — distinguish                                |
|                                                                       |
| If intoxication was involuntary (e.g. spiked drink), it is not caught |
| by the Majewski rule and may negative the mental element, or found a  |
| defence in itself. Rare in practice — anticipate and close it off in  |
| interview.                                                            |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

(d) Self-defence

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| Self-defence — the "unlawful violence" limb                           |
|                                                                       |
| Affray requires that the violence used/threatened be unlawful. Lawful |
| self-defence (common law, and s.18 Non-Fatal Offences Against the     |
| Person Act 1997 / Criminal Law Act 1976 framework) renders force      |
| lawful and therefore takes the accused's conduct outside the offence. |
|                                                                       |
| Belief in the need to defend is judged on the facts as the accused    |
| honestly believed them to be (R v Owino [1995] Crim LR 743,           |
| persuasive; and see the Irish honest-belief line). But the degree of  |
| force must be reasonable/proportionate. A person who is the           |
| aggressor, or who continues after the threat has passed, cannot rely  |
| on it. Voluntary intoxication does not entitle an accused to rely on  |
| a mistaken belief in the need for self-defence.                       |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

5. Charging Decisions — Affray vs. Alternatives

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
  Offence                 When to prefer it over affray
  ----------------------- -----------------------------------------------
  s.6 CJ(PO) 1994 —       Lower-level single-actor public disorder; no
  threatening/abusive     genuine "fight" or scene of terror. Summary.
  behaviour

  s.2 / s.3 Non-Fatal     Violence targeted at one identifiable victim
  Offences 1997 — assault with identifiable injury, and no wider group
                          melee putting bystanders in fear. s.3 needs
                          harm.

  s.18 CJ(PO) 1994 —      Assault with intent to cause bodily harm /
  assault with intent     commit an indictable offence — a serious
                          single-target assault.

  s.14 CJ(PO) 1994 — riot 12 or more persons using/threatening unlawful
                          violence for a common purpose. Higher
                          threshold; reserve for large-scale disorder.

  s.15 CJ(PO) 1994 —      3 or more persons using/threatening unlawful
  violent disorder        violence. Sits between affray and riot — often
                          the better charge for a multi-handed
                          disturbance where numbers ≥ 3.
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

Practical steer: for a two-hander fight in public that puts onlookers in
fear, affray fits. For a three-plus disturbance, actively consider s.15
violent disorder — it is frequently the more natural charge and carries
up to 10 years. Match the charge to the number of participants and the
quality of fear generated.

6. Investigation — Evidence to Gather

At scene / first response

-   Preserve the scene; note positions, weapons, damage. Photograph
    before it is cleared.

-   Identify and separate participants; identify independent
    witnesses/bystanders — their fear/reaction is gold for the
    s.16(1)(c) limb.

-   Record who was fighting whom, and the nature of the violence (blows,
    kicks, weapons, thrown objects).

-   Seize CCTV early — issue preservation requests to
    premises/DCC/transport operators the same day. CCTV showing the
    melee and bystanders scattering is often the strongest single
    exhibit.

-   Note intoxication observations (to pre-empt an intoxication
    argument), and any first accounts / spontaneous admissions.

Building the file

-   Witness statements from: independent bystanders (fear element),
    members present, injured parties, and Garda members.

-   CCTV/body-worn footage — continuity statements and
    technical/download statements.

-   Medical evidence for any injuries (supports the reality of the
    violence and any alternative assault counts).

-   Custody record, memo of interview, and any admissions.

-   Map/plan of locus; photographs of damage; PULSE incident linkage.

Interview strategy

-   Establish the accused's presence and participation — did they use or
    threaten violence.

-   Close off self-defence: who started it, was the threat over, was
    force proportionate.

-   Address intoxication: voluntary intoxication is no defence — but
    record the account for completeness and to rebut
    involuntary-intoxication claims.

-   Explore awareness/intent — that they knew their conduct was or
    threatened violence.

7. Anticipated Defences & Rebuttals

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "It was just words / I made threats but did nothing."                 |
|                                                                       |
| s.16(2): a threat by words alone won't found affray — but check the   |
| conduct. If there was menacing physical conduct accompanying the      |
| words, the offence stands. If truly words only, consider s.6 instead. |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "I was defending myself."                                             |
|                                                                       |
| Test honest belief + proportionality. Establish who was the aggressor |
| and whether force continued after any threat passed. If the accused's |
| violence was unlawful, the limb is met.                               |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "I was too drunk to know what I was doing."                           |
|                                                                       |
| Basic-intent offence — voluntary intoxication is no defence           |
| (Majewski, applied in Ireland). Rebut with observations of purposeful |
| conduct on CCTV.                                                      |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "No one was actually put in fear / no bystander present."             |
|                                                                       |
| s.16(3): no bystander need be present — the test is the notional      |
| person of reasonable firmness. Lead bystander/CCTV evidence to show   |
| the objective quality of fear the combined conduct would produce.     |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| "The violence was only aimed at one person."                          |
|                                                                       |
| If focused solely on one victim with no wider scene of terror         |
| (Leeson, persuasive), affray may fail — but that usually means an     |
| assault charge fits better, not an acquittal. Charge in the           |
| alternative where appropriate.                                        |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

8. Quick Reference Card

+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+
| s.16 AFFRAY — AT A GLANCE                                             |
|                                                                       |
| -   Statute: s.16 CJ(PO) Act 1994. Common law affray abolished        |
|     (s.16(5)).                                                        |
|                                                                       |
| -   Persons: 2 or more, using/threatening violence towards each       |
|     other.                                                            |
|                                                                       |
| -   Place: public or private.                                         |
|                                                                       |
| -   Threat: not by words alone (s.16(2)).                             |
|                                                                       |
| -   Bystander: notional — none need be present (s.16(3)).             |
|                                                                       |
| -   Test: combined conduct would make a person of reasonable firmness |
|     fear for own/another's safety.                                    |
|                                                                       |
| -   Mens rea: basic intent — voluntary intoxication no defence.       |
|                                                                       |
| -   Penalty: indictable; up to 5 years and/or fine. "Serious offence" |
|     under Bail Act 1997.                                              |
|                                                                       |
| -   Consider instead: s.15 violent disorder (3+), s.14 riot (12+),    |
|     s.6, s.2/3 assault, s.18.                                         |
+-----------------------------------------------------------------------+

live file. English POA 1986 authorities are persuasive only.
